topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The glossy brochure for the new city, with its sprawling skyline and promise of 'unlimited opportunities,' lay crumpled on my desk. Every word felt like a deliberate jab. Sarah, my anchor, the person who knew the rhythm of my entire existence, was packing boxes for a life thousands of miles away. I stared at the packing tape, the adhesive looking suddenly menacing. My hands started to tremble, a slight, involuntary shaking that I desperately tried to mask by gripping the edge of the desk until my knuckles turned white. When she finally called, her voice bright and cheerful, describing the sunlight on her new apartment balcony, I managed a thin, tight smile that didn't reach my eyes. "That sounds amazing, Sarah," I said, the words sounding brittle, like old glass about to shatter. Inside, a cold knot tightened in my stomach, and I focused intently on the dust motes dancing in the afternoon light, wishing the floor would simply swallow me whole.

[story 2]

I watched Sarah load the last of her books into the moving van. The sheer volume of her belongings seemed to mock the emptiness that was rapidly opening up in my own space. A strange, hollow sensation settled deep in my chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. When she looked over, her expression was one of pure, unburdened excitement, and that contrast was a physical blow. I cleared my throat, a dry, rasping sound, and forced my posture to straighten, a brittle defense mechanism kicking in. "So, this is... big," I managed, my voice pitched a little too high. I immediately looked down at my shoes, focusing on the scuff marks, desperate to avoid meeting her gaze. The silence that followed felt cavernous, filled only with the sound of my own frantic pulse drumming against my ribs.

[story 3]

The text message arrived: 'Leaving next week! So excited for this adventure!' The notification popped up, and I felt a sudden, sharp clench behind my sternum. I picked up my phone, the screen reflecting a distorted image of my own face—pale, drawn, with shadows under my eyes that no amount of concealer could hide. I paced the length of my small living room, the floorboards protesting beneath my hurried steps. Each movement felt exaggerated, clumsy. When I finally dialed her number, my voice came out strangely flat, devoid of its usual warmth. "Wow," I articulated slowly, letting the word hang in the air like an accusation. I could hear her laughter on the other end, easy and carefree, and the sound only amplified the profound sense of displacement settling over me.
